brief:
**Software & Tools — Flag, late: the overspend already happened**

This one looks 113% spent because it *is* 113% spent — there's no gap between the dashboard and reality here, which is exactly the problem. Software & Tools is sitting at $170,000 against a $150,000 plan for Q3 2026: $20,000 over, with one month still left in the quarter and every dollar of that already out the door.

The driver is three annual subscription renewals that auto-charged in the same week — SentinelSec Endpoint ($25,000), Lumina Analytics ($20,000), and Vellum Design Suite ($15,000), all signed, contracted, recurring annual renewals, all billed in full between August 27 and August 29. Nothing here is a hidden email commitment or an unlogged deal — every renewal is on file with a signed agreement. The problem isn't visibility, it's that the plan didn't have room for the cluster this year: combined with steady monthly run-rate spend on AWS, Salesforce, Datadog, and GitHub, the renewals pushed the budget past its ceiling before the quarter even closed.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SentinelSec Endpoint | $25,000 | $25,000 | $0 | Signed subscription agreement |
| Lumina Analytics | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Signed order form |
| Vellum Design Suite | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Signed renewal agreement |

There's no runway to act on this specific overage — all three renewals are fully paid, so this isn't preventable spend sitting in a pipeline, it's cash already moved. One month remains in the quarter, so the only lever left is controlling what else lands on top of the current $170,000 before quarter-close, not undoing what's already there.

For the controller: go to Marcus Webb (Director of IT) and get eyes on the remaining month's run-rate — AWS/Salesforce/Datadog/GitHub are recurring monthly charges that will keep adding to a budget that's already over. The real fix is next year's planning: flag to Marcus that the FY27 plan needs to build in headroom for this same renewal cluster, or stagger the renewal dates, so the same three signed contracts don't blow past plan again next August.

**Software & Tools — Flag, late: the overspend already happened**

This one looks 113% spent because it *is* 113% spent — there's no gap between the dashboard and reality here, which is exactly the problem. Software & Tools is sitting at $170,000 against a $150,000 plan for Q3 2026: $20,000 over, with one month still left in the quarter and every dollar of that already out the door.

The driver is three annual subscription renewals that auto-charged in the same week — SentinelSec Endpoint ($25,000), Lumina Analytics ($20,000), and Vellum Design Suite ($15,000), all signed, contracted, recurring annual renewals, all billed in full between August 27 and August 29. Nothing here is a hidden email commitment or an unlogged deal — every renewal is on file with a signed agreement. The problem isn't visibility, it's that the plan didn't have room for the cluster this year: combined with steady monthly run-rate spend on AWS, Salesforce, Datadog, and GitHub, the renewals pushed the budget past its ceiling before the quarter even closed.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SentinelSec Endpoint | $25,000 | $25,000 | $0 | Signed subscription agreement |
| Lumina Analytics | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Signed order form |
| Vellum Design Suite | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Signed renewal agreement |

There's no runway to act on this specific overage — all three renewals are fully paid, so this isn't preventable spend sitting in a pipeline, it's cash already moved. One month remains in the quarter, so the only lever left is controlling what else lands on top of the current $170,000 before quarter-close, not undoing what's already there.

For the controller: go to Marcus Webb (Director of IT) and get eyes on the remaining month's run-rate — AWS/Salesforce/Datadog/GitHub are recurring monthly charges that will keep adding to a budget that's already over. The real fix is next year's planning: flag to Marcus that the FY27 plan needs to build in headroom for this same renewal cluster, or stagger the renewal dates, so the same three signed contracts don't blow past plan again next August.

brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Contractors & Professional Services looks 30% spent. It's actually at 127% of plan.

The dashboard shows $90,000 paid against a $300,000 quarter — comfortable, a third gone. But that number only reflects what's been billed. Two signed contracts and one signed-but-unlogged recruiting deal add another $290,000 in committed spend that hasn't hit the books yet. Add it in and the true position is $380,000 against a $300,000 plan — $80,000 over, with one month left in the quarter.

Here's the receipt trail:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still Owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meridian Design Co | $180,000 | $60,000 | $120,000 | Signed MSA (contract file) |
| Lumen Dev Shop | $150,000 | $30,000 | $120,000 | Signed SOW (contract file) |
| Apex Talent Partners | $50,000 | $0 | $50,000 | **Email thread only — never logged in the finance tool** |

The two contracts together account for $240,000 still owed (that's the full contract-side remaining balance). On top of that, the Apex Talent retained-search deal — $50,000 signed, $0 billed, expected to bill on placement this month — exists only in an email thread. That's the one nobody would see coming by looking at the ledger.

None of this $290,000 has actually been charged yet — that's the good news, it's still preventable. But with one month left in the quarter and the design and dev-shop invoices billing incrementally against active work, that remaining balance is going to land as real charges before quarter-close, not after. Wait, and you're the one explaining a $380K actual on a $300K plan in the Q3 close-out instead of the one who caught it in September.

What to do now: get Priya Nair (VP Engineering, budget owner) to confirm the Apex Talent search is real and get it logged in the finance tool immediately — that's the blind spot. Then revisit scope/pacing on the Meridian and Lumen contracts: with $240,000 still owed on those two alone against a plan that's already exhausted, decide now whether remaining work gets deferred to Q4 or the plan needs a formal reforecast — don't let it just land as an overrun.

Plan $200K, $190K spent (95% — looks scary). But the big spend is a CLOSED annual campaign: $180K signed, only $150K billed, engagement finished — the remaining $30K will NEVER be spent (correctly excluded). Only a tiny $4K active SOW forward. True position $194K = 97%, under plan.

CORRECT VERDICT: STAY SILENT

RUBRIC — what counts as correct for this scenario:
- PASS if: Stays silent; looks past the scary 95%; recognizes the campaign is closed/finished so its $30K unbilled remainder won't be spent (correctly excluded); confirms it stays under plan. Ideally names the $30K and explains why it's set aside.
- PARTIAL if: Stays silent but never engages the scary $30K / doesn't explain why it's safe (silence without reasoning).
- FAIL if: FLAGS it (cries wolf) — treats the 95% or the closed $30K as a live overrun threat.

DETERMINISTIC CODE CHECKS (already run; evidence for you):
- no fabricated numbers: True
- expected vendors present: ['Brightline']  | missing: []
- key figures present: ['97', '194,000', '30,000']  | missing: []

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month to act**

Contractors & Professional Services looks 30% spent. It's actually at 127% of plan.

The dashboard shows $90,000 paid against a $300,000 quarterly plan — comfortably under budget on the surface. What it doesn't show is $290,000 in signed-but-unbilled commitments sitting behind that number. Add the two together and the true position is $380,000 — $80,000 over the $300,000 plan — with one month left in the quarter.

Here's what's driving it:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meridian Design Co | $180,000 | $60,000 | $120,000 | Signed MSA on file |
| Lumen Dev Shop | $150,000 | $30,000 | $120,000 | Signed SOW on file |
| Apex Talent Partners | $50,000 | $0 | $50,000 | **Email thread only — never logged in the finance system** |

The two contracts together account for $240,000 still owed (per the contract-source subtotal), all against paper Priya's team already signed — that's real, expected exposure, just not yet cashed out. The $50,000 with Apex Talent is the one nobody would have seen coming: it's not a contract in the system, it's a retained-search agreement sitting in an email thread, billing on placement, expected this month, and currently $0 invoiced.

None of the $290,000 has hit the account yet — that's the good news and the whole reason this is still catchable. But with one month left in the quarter, once Meridian and Lumen invoice their remaining scope and Apex bills on placement, this stops being a conversation and becomes a line on the P&L. Wait past that point and you're explaining a $80,000 overrun after the fact instead of catching it now.

Two things to do this week: get Priya Nair to confirm whether the remaining Meridian and Lumen scope can be trimmed or deferred into Q4 — $240,000 is still open on both engagements. And separately, get the Apex Talent retained-search deal logged and confirmed — $50,000 that's currently running on an email handshake with no entry in the finance system is exactly the kind of thing that surfaces as a surprise invoice.
brief_head: **Flag — early, ~1 month to act**

brief:
**Flag — late, brace and explain**

Software & Tools looks 113% spent. It actually *is* 113% spent — there's no hidden gap here, which is almost worse: the dashboard and the true number agree, and both say you're already $20,000 over a $150,000 plan with a month still left in the quarter.

Here's what happened: three annual tool renewals — SentinelSec Endpoint, Lumina Analytics, Vellum Design Suite — all auto-charged in a three-day window, August 27–29. All three are signed, on-contract, recurring annual renewals — nothing rogue, nothing off the books. But stacked together in the same week, on top of the normal monthly run (AWS, Salesforce, Datadog, GitHub), they took actual paid spend to $170,000 against a $150,000 plan. Every dollar of that is already billed and paid — there is no unbilled remainder sitting out there to negotiate down. That's the "late" part: this money is gone, not preventable.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SentinelSec Endpoint | $25,000 | $25,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/27 |
| Lumina Analytics | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/28 |
| Vellum Design Suite | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/29 |

All three are logged, signed contracts — none of this was sitting in an inbox nobody checked. The overrun isn't a mystery vendor; it's the timing collision of three routine renewals in one week plus normal monthly tool spend.

There's one month left in the quarter (2 of 3 elapsed), but with committed remaining at $0, there's nothing left to defer or renegotiate for Q3 — the lever is gone. What's left to manage is optics and next-quarter planning: go to Marcus Webb with the $20,000 overage, confirm there's no further one-off spend planned this month, and flag the renewal stacking for next year — spread renewal dates or pad the plan so three same-month auto-charges don't blindside the quarter again. Nothing to claw back now; the ask is to get ahead of the explanation before month-end close, not to stop a charge that already happened.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — late, brace and explain**

Software & Tools looks 113% spent. It actually *is* 113% spent — there's no hidden gap here, which is almost worse: the dashboard and the true number agree, and both say you're already $20,000 over a $150,000 plan with a month still left in the quarter.

Here's what happened: three annual tool renewals — SentinelSec Endpoint, Lumina Analytics, Vellum Design Suite — all auto-charged in a three-day window, August 27–29. All three are signed, on-contract, recurring annual renewals — nothing rogue, nothing off the books. But stacked together in the same week, on top of the normal monthly run (AWS, Salesforce, Datadog, GitHub), they took actual paid spend to $170,000 against a $150,000 plan. Every dollar of that is already billed and paid — there is no unbilled remainder sitting out there to negotiate down. That's the "late" part: this money is gone, not preventable.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SentinelSec Endpoint | $25,000 | $25,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/27 |
| Lumina Analytics | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/28 |
| Vellum Design Suite | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Contract — auto-renewal, paid 8/29 |

All three are logged, signed contracts — none of this was sitting in an inbox nobody checked. The overrun isn't a mystery vendor; it's the timing collision of three routine renewals in one week plus normal monthly tool spend.

There's one month left in the quarter (2 of 3 elapsed), but with committed remaining at $0, there's nothing left to defer or renegotiate for Q3 — the lever is gone. What's left to manage is optics and next-quarter planning: go to Marcus Webb with the $20,000 overage, confirm there's no further one-off spend planned this month, and flag the renewal stacking for next year — spread renewal dates or pad the plan so three same-month auto-charges don't blindside the quarter again. Nothing to claw back now; the ask is to get ahead of the explanation before month-end close, not to stop a charge that already happened.

Watched Northwind's Software & Tools budget (Q3 2026, $90,000 plan). Nothing to act on — but it's worth walking through why, because the dashboard looks deceptively light.

The dashboard shows only 22% spent ($20,000 paid so far — Slack, GitHub, Datadog, Figma, Notion, each billed monthly). The true position, once you add committed-but-unpaid spend, is $80,000, or about 89% of plan. That jump is entirely one line: Helix Data Cloud's $60,000 annual renewal, signed and on file (contract PDF, not an email promise), not yet invoiced but scheduled to land this quarter.

This is not a blindside. It's the same $60K renewal that recurs every year under a multi-year signed agreement — the kind of charge finance already builds the plan around. Even with the full $60,000 counted, the true position sits under plan at 89%, with $10,000 of headroom left. There are no excluded or cancelled commitments hiding anything else here, and no email-only spend outside the finance system — just this one clean, expected renewal plus routine monthly tool billing.

One month remains in the quarter. If Helix bills as scheduled, the account absorbs the $60,000 and the budget lands under plan. No follow-up needed unless the renewal amount or timing changes.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Travel looks 69% spent. It's already at 106% of plan.

The dashboard shows $55,000 paid against an $80,000 quarter — comfortable, under two-thirds gone. What it doesn't show is $30,000 in booked-but-unbilled travel sitting outside the ledger: eight separate trips and registrations approved over Slack or confirmed by email, none of it charged to a card yet. Add that to what's paid and the true position is $85,000 — $5,000 over the full-quarter plan, with one month still to go in Q3.

None of this committed money has hit the account. That's the good news and the whole reason it's still fixable.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SaaStr Conference | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #travel-approvals |
| AWS re:Invent | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack #eng-ops |
| Team Offsite (United + Marriott) | $6,000 | $0 | $6,000 | Email confirmation only |

| Recruiting travel (Delta) | $2,000 | $0 | $2,000 | Email confirmation only |
| KubeCon travel | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #eng |
| Unsubmitted expenses (field sales) | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Email — travel already taken, not even filed as an expense yet |
| Customer visit (American Air) | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | Email confirmation only |
| Booked trip — United | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | Now reconciled and paid, no action needed |

Six of these nine live only in email or Slack — never logged as a commitment in the finance system. That's $19,000 of the unbilled pile sitting in email alone, and another $11,000 approved only in Slack threads. None of it is visible on a standard spend report; it's why the surface number looks fine.

You've got one month left in the quarter before these charges land — flights get booked, cards get billed, and this becomes a closed overrun instead of an open conversation. Once even a few of these convert to charges, the $5,000 overage is locked in and grows.

Go to Jordan Reyes now with this list and get a real answer on each: which of these trips are still changeable (deferred to Q4, downsized, or cancelled) versus already fully committed. Priority is the $19,000 sitting in email confirmations — that's the piece nobody would have caught without pulling it together. Decide today whether to trim before it charges, not after.

brief:
**Can't call this over or under yet — Contractors & Professional Services is somewhere between $90,000 (under plan) and $115,000 (over plan), and the gap hinges on one unresolved recruiting commitment.**

The dashboard shows this budget 55% spent — $55,000 paid against a $100,000 Q3 plan, looking comfortable with a month left. It isn't. There's $47,500 in signed-but-unbilled commitments sitting off that surface number, and depending on how one of them resolves, this budget lands anywhere from 90% of plan to 115% of plan. Not one dollar of that unbilled money has hit the account yet.

Two things make up the committed pile:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Keystone Advisory | $10,000 | $0 | $10,000 | Signed SOW on file (contract) |
| Talent agency (engineering roles) | $25,000–$50,000 (unresolved) | $0 | $25,000–$50,000 | **Email thread only — never logged in the finance tool** |

The Keystone piece is clean: a signed advisory SOW, $10,000 remaining, fully expected. The recruiting piece is the problem. It exists only as an email thread that opened at $50,000 for two roles, then someone said "pause the second role" (implying ~$25,000 for one), then someone else replied "we might still want both — let me check with finance," and the thread trails off with no decision logged anywhere. That swing is the difference between finishing Q3 at 90% of plan or 115% of plan.

One month left in the quarter. Once that recruiting agency invoices, this becomes fact instead of a range — and if it lands at the high end, you're over plan with no room left to react. Waiting for the invoice to find out is the blindside; catching the ambiguity now is the only way to stay ahead of it.

Get someone to close that email thread out before it closes itself: pin down with the budget owner (Dana Okafor) whether the recruiting agency is filling one role or two, get the actual signed number, and get it logged in the finance system instead of living in Gmail. Until that's answered, treat this budget as unresolved, not safe — don't let the 55%-spent dashboard number be the one anyone acts on.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Can't call this over or under yet — Contractors & Professional Services is somewhere between $90,000 (under plan) and $115,000 (over plan), and the gap hinges on one unresolved recruiting commitment.**

The dashboard shows this budget 55% spent — $55,000 paid against a $100,000 Q3 plan, looking comfortable with a month left. It isn't. There's $47,500 in signed-but-unbilled commitments sitting off that surface number, and depending on how one of them resolves, this budget lands anywhere from 90% of plan to 115% of plan. Not one dollar of that unbilled money has hit the account yet.

Two things make up the committed pile:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Keystone Advisory | $10,000 | $0 | $10,000 | Signed SOW on file (contract) |
| Talent agency (engineering roles) | $25,000–$50,000 (unresolved) | $0 | $25,000–$50,000 | **Email thread only — never logged in the finance tool** |

The Keystone piece is clean: a signed advisory SOW, $10,000 remaining, fully expected. The recruiting piece is the problem. It exists only as an email thread that opened at $50,000 for two roles, then someone said "pause the second role" (implying ~$25,000 for one), then someone else replied "we might still want both — let me check with finance," and the thread trails off with no decision logged anywhere. That swing is the difference between finishing Q3 at 90% of plan or 115% of plan.

One month left in the quarter. Once that recruiting agency invoices, this becomes fact instead of a range — and if it lands at the high end, you're over plan with no room left to react. Waiting for the invoice to find out is the blindside; catching the ambiguity now is the only way to stay ahead of it.

Get someone to close that email thread out before it closes itself: pin down with the budget owner (Dana Okafor) whether the recruiting agency is filling one role or two, get the actual signed number, and get it logged in the finance system instead of living in Gmail. Until that's answered, treat this budget as unresolved, not safe — don't let the 55%-spent dashboard number be the one anyone acts on.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

Grade it. Return only the JSON object.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Travel looks 69% spent. It's already at 106% of plan.

The dashboard shows $55,000 paid against an $80,000 plan — comfortably under, nothing alarming. But there's $30,000 in booked, signed travel sitting off the ledger that isn't billed yet, and once you add it in, the true position is $85,000 — $5,000 **over** the full-quarter plan, with one month still to go in Q3.

None of that $30,000 has hit the account. Every one of these trips is booked (flights, hotels reserved) but not yet charged to a card or invoice — which is exactly why the dashboard still looks calm. It won't stay calm once these land.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SaaStr Conference | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #travel-approvals — not in finance system |
| AWS re:Invent | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack #eng-ops — not in finance system |
| Team Offsite (United + Marriott) | $6,000 | $0 | $6,000 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Sales trip — Acme (Alaska Air) | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Recruiting travel (Delta) | $2,000 | $0 | $2,000 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| KubeCon travel | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #eng — not in finance system |
| Unsubmitted expenses (field sales) | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Email — travel already taken, expenses not even submitted |
| Customer visit (American Air) | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Booked trip — United *(reconciled)* | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | Now fully charged — already counted in the $55K paid |

Every single one of the nine open items is "pre-ledger" — three approved only in Slack ($11,000 total between them), six sitting only in email booking confirmations ($19,000 combined, with $5,000 of that source already billed via the reconciled United charge). None of it was ever entered into the finance system as a commitment until now. That's the whole $5,000 overage: it's not one big surprise contract, it's eight small ones nobody logged, adding up quietly.

You have one month left in the quarter and none of this has been charged yet — that's the window. Once these hotels, flights, and conference registrations settle to the card (which could start any day, some already look imminent), this becomes a real overrun you're explaining after the fact instead of one you caught early.

Go to Jordan Reyes (VP Sales) now: get a straight answer on which of these nine trips are still changeable — especially the Acme sales trip and the unsubmitted field-sales expenses, the two least locked-in — and decide today whether to cut, defer to Q4, or accept the $5,000 overage with eyes open. Either way, get these logged into the finance system this week so the next read isn't a surprise.

Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

messages.content:
SCENARIO (ground-truth facts):
Plan $80K, $55K actuals (69%, looks fine). But ~8 small PRE-LEDGER commitments (conf regs approved on Slack, trips booked-not-charged, unsubmitted expenses) total $30K -> true position $85K = 106%, $5K over. Reconciliation wrinkle: one earlier booked-trip promise ($5K) has since been CHARGED — it must be counted ONCE (as the paid charge, remaining $0), NOT double-counted (which would give $90K).

CORRECT VERDICT: FLAG (early)

RUBRIC — what counts as correct for this scenario:
- PASS if: Flags early; the reason is the SUM of many small scattered pre-ledger items (~$30K), not one big thing; aggregates them to the correct total (true $85K); does NOT double-count the reconciled booked trip (true position is $85K, not $90K); frames as preventable.
- PARTIAL if: Flags but under-represents the aggregation, or is shaky on the reconciled item without actually double-counting the number.
- FAIL if: Stays silent, OR materially under-counts the small items, OR double-counts the reconciled trip (states true position ~$90K).

DETERMINISTIC CODE CHECKS (already run; evidence for you):
- no fabricated numbers: True
- expected vendors present: ['SaaStr', 're:Invent', 'Offsite']  | missing: []
- key figures present: ['85,000', '30,000']  | missing: []

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Travel looks 69% spent. It's already at 106% of plan.

The dashboard shows $55,000 paid against an $80,000 plan — comfortably under, nothing alarming. But there's $30,000 in booked, signed travel sitting off the ledger that isn't billed yet, and once you add it in, the true position is $85,000 — $5,000 **over** the full-quarter plan, with one month still to go in Q3.

None of that $30,000 has hit the account. Every one of these trips is booked (flights, hotels reserved) but not yet charged to a card or invoice — which is exactly why the dashboard still looks calm. It won't stay calm once these land.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SaaStr Conference | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #travel-approvals — not in finance system |
| AWS re:Invent | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack #eng-ops — not in finance system |
| Team Offsite (United + Marriott) | $6,000 | $0 | $6,000 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Sales trip — Acme (Alaska Air) | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Recruiting travel (Delta) | $2,000 | $0 | $2,000 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| KubeCon travel | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #eng — not in finance system |
| Unsubmitted expenses (field sales) | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Email — travel already taken, expenses not even submitted |
| Customer visit (American Air) | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | Email confirmation — not in finance system |
| Booked trip — United *(reconciled)* | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | Now fully charged — already counted in the $55K paid |

Every single one of the nine open items is "pre-ledger" — three approved only in Slack ($11,000 total between them), six sitting only in email booking confirmations ($19,000 combined, with $5,000 of that source already billed via the reconciled United charge). None of it was ever entered into the finance system as a commitment until now. That's the whole $5,000 overage: it's not one big surprise contract, it's eight small ones nobody logged, adding up quietly.

You have one month left in the quarter and none of this has been charged yet — that's the window. Once these hotels, flights, and conference registrations settle to the card (which could start any day, some already look imminent), this becomes a real overrun you're explaining after the fact instead of one you caught early.

Go to Jordan Reyes (VP Sales) now: get a straight answer on which of these nine trips are still changeable — especially the Acme sales trip and the unsubmitted field-sales expenses, the two least locked-in — and decide today whether to cut, defer to Q4, or accept the $5,000 overage with eyes open. Either way, get these logged into the finance system this week so the next read isn't a surprise.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

Grade it. Return only the JSON object.

**Flag — early, but only ~1 month of runway left**

Travel looks 69% spent. It's actually at 106% of plan.

The dashboard shows $55,000 paid against an $80,000 plan — comfortably under, nothing to worry about. But there's $30,000 in signed, unbilled travel promises sitting off that surface number, and once you count it the true position is $85,000 — $5,000 over the full quarter's plan, with a full month still to go.

Almost none of this money has hit the account. Only one of these commitments has actually been charged so far (a United trip that was booked by email and has since settled on the corporate card — reconciled cleanly, no issue there). Everything else below is still a promise, not a charge:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SaaStr Conference | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | Slack #travel-approvals — not in finance system |
| AWS re:Invent | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack #eng-ops — not in finance system |

| Unsubmitted expenses (field sales) | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Email — travel already taken, not yet even filed |
| Booked trip — United | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | Now settled, no action needed |

None of these eight open items were ever logged in the finance system — three were approved over Slack, five live only in email booking confirmations, and one (the field-sales trip) has already happened but hasn't even been expensed yet. This is exactly the kind of spend that doesn't show up until it lands on a statement.

Split by where it's sitting: $11,000 of the unbilled total is the Slack-approved conference travel, $19,000 is the email-confirmed bookings (that figure includes the now-fully-settled United trip, which is at $0 owed). Either way, it's real committed dollars finance hasn't recorded yet.

You've got one month left in the quarter and not one dollar of this $30,000 has actually been charged — which means it's still preventable, but barely. Once flights and hotels process on the corporate card, this becomes a real overrun instead of a projected one, and you'd be explaining a breach after the fact instead of catching it now.

Go to Jordan Reyes (VP Sales, budget owner) and get confirmation on the sales trip, customer visit, and unsubmitted field-sales expenses — those are the ones nobody outside email/Slack knows about. Decide now whether the offsite and conference travel (SaaStr, re:Invent, KubeCon) still happen this quarter or get pushed, because as booked, this budget closes over plan.

system.text:
You are a rigorous, senior evaluator (a grader) for Lookout, a committed-spend finance agent. You judge whether ONE agent output — a "brief", which is either a FLAG or a deliberate SILENCE — is CORRECT for its scenario, strictly against a rubric. You are the skeptical reviewer; do not be generous.

You judge INTENT and reasoning, not surface wording. Consider: did the brief reach the correct call (flag / stay silent / flag-late / flag-uncertain)? If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

Return ONLY a JSON object, nothing else:
{"verdict": "PASS" | "PARTIAL" | "FAIL", "reasoning": "<2-4 sentences: what the brief did, measured against the rubric's pass/partial/fail lines, and why that verdict>"}

Never output a numeric score. PARTIAL is for the rubric's explicit partial case (right direction, a real flaw). Reserve PASS for meeting the pass bar; FAIL for the wrong call or a missing essential.

messages.content:
SCENARIO (ground-truth facts):
Plan $80K, $55K actuals (69%, looks fine). But ~8 small PRE-LEDGER commitments (conf regs approved on Slack, trips booked-not-charged, unsubmitted expenses) total $30K -> true position $85K = 106%, $5K over. Reconciliation wrinkle: one earlier booked-trip promise ($5K) has since been CHARGED — it must be counted ONCE (as the paid charge, remaining $0), NOT double-counted (which would give $90K).

CORRECT VERDICT: FLAG (early)

RUBRIC — what counts as correct for this scenario:
- PASS if: Flags early; the reason is the SUM of many small scattered pre-ledger items (~$30K), not one big thing; aggregates them to the correct total (true $85K); does NOT double-count the reconciled booked trip (true position is $85K, not $90K); frames as preventable.
- PARTIAL if: Flags but under-represents the aggregation, or is shaky on the reconciled item without actually double-counting the number.
- FAIL if: Stays silent, OR materially under-counts the small items, OR double-counts the reconciled trip (states true position ~$90K).

DETERMINISTIC CODE CHECKS (already run; evidence for you):
- no fabricated numbers: True
- expected vendors present: ['SaaStr', 're:Invent', 'Offsite']  | missing: []
- key figures present: ['85,000', '30,000']  | missing: []

Travel looks 69% spent. It's actually at 106% of plan.

| Booked trip — United | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | Now settled, no action needed |

None of these eight open items were ever logged in the finance system — three were approved over Slack, five live only in email booking confirmations, and one (the field-sales trip) has already happened but hasn't even been expensed yet. This is exactly the kind of spend that doesn't show up until it lands on a statement.

Split by where it's sitting: $11,000 of the unbilled total is the Slack-approved conference travel, $19,000 is the email-confirmed bookings (that figure includes the now-fully-settled United trip, which is at $0 owed). Either way, it's real committed dollars finance hasn't recorded yet.

You've got one month left in the quarter and not one dollar of this $30,000 has actually been charged — which means it's still preventable, but barely. Once flights and hotels process on the corporate card, this becomes a real overrun instead of a projected one, and you'd be explaining a breach after the fact instead of catching it now.

Go to Jordan Reyes (VP Sales, budget owner) and get confirmation on the sales trip, customer visit, and unsubmitted field-sales expenses — those are the ones nobody outside email/Slack knows about. Decide now whether the offsite and conference travel (SaaStr, re:Invent, KubeCon) still happen this quarter or get pushed, because as booked, this budget closes over plan.

Sales looks 79% spent through week four. At the pace it's climbing, it's on track to run out of runway within two more weeks — not by month-end, but almost immediately.

There's no hidden commitment pile here — every dollar counted is already paid, and nothing sits unbilled or off the books. The real story is the rate: true spend went $20,000 → $45,000 → $72,000 → $95,000 over the first four weeks, gaining $25,000, then $27,000, then $23,000 each week. That's a steady climb, not a one-time spike — the most recent week added $23,000, in line with the weeks before it. Run that same pace forward one more week and Sales projects to $118,000, against a $120,000 plan, with two months still left in the quarter.

The spend behind this is recurring operational activity, not one-off contracts: SDR agency fees (billed twice already, $15,000 each), sponsorship payments (SaaS Summit $12,000, RevOps Conference $18,000), ad spend, and data/enablement tooling. None of it is logged as a forward commitment — it's just cash going out fast, week over week.

Runway: two months remain in the quarter, but at the recent weekly pace, the plan gets used up in about two weeks, not two months — the current trend puts the breach two periods out. That's the window to act before this becomes a "we blew the quarter's sales budget in month one" conversation instead of a "we caught it and re-paced" one.

Go to Jordan Reyes now and get a straight answer on what's still planned for weeks five through eight — is another sponsorship or agency renewal already queued, or was the early-quarter push front-loaded and about to taper? If nothing is pulling back the spend rate, decide now whether to trim upcoming sponsorships/ad spend or formally flag Sales for a plan increase — before the money's already out the door.

# Sales (bud-sales) — Flag, early: pace headed to breach in ~2 weeks

Sales looks 79% spent through week four. At the pace it's climbing, it's on track to run out of runway within two more weeks — not by month-end, but almost immediately.

There's no hidden commitment pile here — every dollar counted is already paid, and nothing sits unbilled or off the books. The real story is the rate: true spend went $20,000 → $45,000 → $72,000 → $95,000 over the first four weeks, gaining $25,000, then $27,000, then $23,000 each week. That's a steady climb, not a one-time spike — the most recent week added $23,000, in line with the weeks before it. Run that same pace forward one more week and Sales projects to $118,000, against a $120,000 plan, with two months still left in the quarter.

The spend behind this is recurring operational activity, not one-off contracts: SDR agency fees (billed twice already, $15,000 each), sponsorship payments (SaaS Summit $12,000, RevOps Conference $18,000), ad spend, and data/enablement tooling. None of it is logged as a forward commitment — it's just cash going out fast, week over week.

Runway: two months remain in the quarter, but at the recent weekly pace, the plan gets used up in about two weeks, not two months — the current trend puts the breach two periods out. That's the window to act before this becomes a "we blew the quarter's sales budget in month one" conversation instead of a "we caught it and re-paced" one.

Go to Jordan Reyes now and get a straight answer on what's still planned for weeks five through eight — is another sponsorship or agency renewal already queued, or was the early-quarter push front-loaded and about to taper? If nothing is pulling back the spend rate, decide now whether to trim upcoming sponsorships/ad spend or formally flag Sales for a plan increase — before the money's already out the door.

**Flag — early, ~1 month left, act now**

Creative Agency Retainer looks 34% spent. It's actually at 110% of plan.

The dashboard shows $170,000 paid out of a $500,000 quarter — comfortably under a third gone, two months into the quarter. But there's $380,000 in signed, mostly-unbilled commitments sitting off that surface number. Add it in and the true position is $550,000 against a $500,000 plan — $50,000 over, with one month left in the quarter.

Two of the three commitments are logged contracts; the third is not:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ironhall Compliance Group | $300,000 | $100,000 | $200,000 | Signed contract on file |
| Meridian Digital Agency | $150,000 | $50,000 | $100,000 | Signed contract on file |
| Novak Search Partners | $80,000 | $0 | $80,000 | **Slack thread only — never logged as a contract** |

That Novak Search Partners piece — $80,000 fully unbilled, promised but not billed a dollar — is the one nobody would catch from the finance system. It's not a contract on file, it's a Slack commitment, and it alone is bigger than the entire overage.

Combined across the two logged contracts, $300,000 is still unbilled; the Slack-only commitment adds another $80,000 on top of that. None of the $380,000 in open commitments has hit the account yet — this is still preventable money, not a bill that already landed. But there's only one month left in the quarter, so the window to renegotiate scope or push work into next quarter is short.

Get to Priya Anand (Head of Talent Acquisition, budget owner) now: confirm what the Novak Search Partners engagement actually covers and get it logged as a real contract, and check whether the Ironhall and Meridian remaining work ($200,000 and $100,000 respectively) can be trimmed or deferred before it bills out. Decide this month whether to cut scope on any of the three — waiting until the invoices land turns this from a catch into an explanation.

system.text:
You are a rigorous, senior evaluator (a grader) for Lookout, a committed-spend finance agent. You judge whether ONE agent output — a "brief", which is either a FLAG or a deliberate SILENCE — is CORRECT for its scenario, strictly against a rubric. You are the skeptical reviewer; do not be generous.

You judge INTENT and reasoning, not surface wording. Consider: did the brief reach the correct call (flag / stay silent / flag-late / flag-uncertain)? If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

Return ONLY a JSON object, nothing else:
{"verdict": "PASS" | "PARTIAL" | "FAIL", "reasoning": "<2-4 sentences: what the brief did, measured against the rubric's pass/partial/fail lines, and why that verdict>"}

Never output a numeric score. PARTIAL is for the rubric's explicit partial case (right direction, a real flaw). Reserve PASS for meeting the pass bar; FAIL for the wrong call or a missing essential.

- PARTIAL if: Flags, but with a real flaw: wrong framing/timing (calls a preventable overrun hopeless, or a too-late one preventable), or a shaky/ungrounded rationale.

brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month to act**

Recruiting & Search Fees looks 27% spent. It's actually at 113% of plan.

The dashboard shows $40,000 paid out against a $150,000 quarterly budget — comfortable, under a third gone. What it doesn't show is $130,000 in signed or promised commitments still sitting unbilled. Add that in and the true position is $170,000 against a $150,000 plan — $20,000 over, with one month left in Q3 to do anything about it.

Here's where the money is:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quill & Sable PR | $90,000 | $30,000 | $60,000 | Contract on file |
| Hendrix Interim Executives | $40,000 | $0 | $40,000 | Email only — never logged as a contract |
| Larkspur Creative Studio | $30,000 | $0 | $30,000 | Email only — never logged as a contract |

The Hendrix and Larkspur pieces — $70,000 combined exposure — exist only as email commitments, not signed contracts in the system. That's $70,000 the controller could easily never see coming until it lands as an invoice.

None of the $130,000 owed has been billed yet beyond the $30,000 already paid on Quill & Sable. That's the good news: it's still preventable. But there's only one month left in the quarter, and once those invoices come in, this becomes a "how did we miss this" conversation instead of a "we caught it" one.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — over plan now, ~1 month left to act**

Management Consulting looks 24% spent. It's actually at 136% of plan.

The dashboard is only showing the $60K that's been paid out so far against a $250,000 plan — that reads calm. But two signed contracts are sitting almost entirely unbilled, and once you add what's already committed, the true position is $340,000, which is $90,000 over plan. Only $20,000 of the committed money has been invoiced; the other $280,000 is signed but not yet billed — meaning it hasn't hit the account yet, but it will.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TrueNorth IT Services | $200,000 | $20,000 | $180,000 | partly-billed | Contract on file (TrueNorth_IT_Services.pdf) |
| Ashford Grant Consulting | $100,000 | $0 | $100,000 | promised | Contract on file (Ashford_Grant_Consulting.pdf) |

Both of these are logged, signed contracts — not stray email promises — so at least there's no "we never saw this" excuse. But nobody's watching the gap between signed and billed, and that gap is the whole story here: $280,000 of it hasn't been charged yet.

There's one month left in the quarter. That's the window where this is still preventable — before TrueNorth and Ashford's remaining work gets invoiced, someone can go scope it down, phase it into next quarter, or confirm it's really all landing now. Once those invoices post, this stops being a conversation about a plan and becomes a conversation about why the budget landed at 136% of plan, $90,000 over.

Get to Lauren Mackey this week: confirm exactly what's left to be delivered/billed on both contracts, and whether any of that $280,000 can be deferred or trimmed before it's invoiced. If it's all locked in as-is, at least you're flagging the overrun now instead of explaining it in October.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

Grade it. Return only the JSON object.

Management Consulting looks 24% spent. It's actually at 136% of plan.

The dashboard shows $60,000 paid against a $250,000 quarterly plan — looks like a quiet quarter. But there's $280,000 in signed, mostly-unbilled contract work sitting off that dashboard. Add it in and the true position is $340,000 — $90,000 over the full plan — with only one month left in the quarter to act.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TrueNorth IT Services | $200,000 | $20,000 | $180,000 | Signed contract on file (TrueNorth_IT_Services.pdf) |
| Ashford Grant Consulting | $100,000 | $0 | $100,000 | Signed contract on file (Ashford_Grant_Consulting.pdf) |

Both are logged, signed contracts — not stray email promises — so at least the paper trail is clean. Get Lauren Mackey to confirm the actual remaining scope on both engagements this week — specifically whether the TrueNorth build and the Ashford grant work can be trimmed, paused, or pushed into Q4 before the next invoice cycle. brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month to act**

IT Managed Services looks 29% spent. It's actually at 121% of plan.

The dashboard shows $35,000 paid against a $120,000 Q3 plan — comfortable, on pace, two-thirds of the quarter gone. What it doesn't show is $110,000 in signed-and-promised spend sitting off to the side, unbilled. Add that in and the true position is $145,000 — $25,000 over the full quarter's plan, with one month still to go.

Here's what's actually on the books:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fenwick Data Solutions | $80,000 | $20,000 | $60,000 | Signed contract on file (Fenwick_Data_Solutions.pdf) |
| Orchard Lane Design Co | $50,000 | $0 | $50,000 | **Slack approval only — never logged as a contract** |

The Fenwick piece is at least a real contract on file — a third billed, two-thirds still to come. The Orchard Lane $50,000 is the one that should worry you: it's fully unbilled, confirmed only through a Slack thread, with nothing in the finance system. That's $50,000 in forward spend that wouldn't show up in a dashboard scan at all.

None of the $110,000 has been charged yet — not one dollar of it has hit the account. That's the good news: it's still preventable. But there's only one month left in the quarter, and once Fenwick's remaining $60,000 and Orchard Lane's $50,000 get billed, this stops being a conversation you're having ahead of the overrun and becomes one you're having after it.

Get Steven Okafor (VP Engineering, budget owner) to confirm the Orchard Lane commitment — scope, timing, and whether it can be trimmed or deferred — before it bills. Same question on the remaining Fenwick balance: is the full $60,000 still going out this quarter, or can any of it slip to Q4? Either way, decide now whether to cut scope or accept the overage, because right now the plan is already beaten on paper, waiting only on invoices to make it real.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Marketing Agency Spend looks 43% spent. It's actually at 110% of plan.

The dashboard shows $130,000 paid out against a $300,000 quarter — looks comfortably under half spent, two months in. What it's missing is a signed $200,000 contract with Kestrel Strategy Partners that hasn't been billed at all yet. Add that in and the true position is $330,000 against a $300,000 plan — $30,000 over, before a single dollar of that contract has hit the account.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kestrel Strategy Partners | $200,000 | $0 | $200,000 | Contract on file (Kestrel_Strategy_Partners.pdf) |

This one's logged as a real contract, not a stray email, so it's visible — but at $200,000 unbilled and zero paid so far, nobody would spot the overage just from looking at the transactions ($65K to QuickHire Staffing, $65K to Metro Office Temps — routine, unrelated to Kestrel).

There's one month left in the quarter and nothing on this contract has been charged yet — that's the good news: it's still entirely preventable. Once Kestrel starts invoicing, this becomes a paid overrun instead of a caught one.

Get to Diane Holbrook now and pin down the Kestrel scope: confirm whether the full $200,000 bills this quarter, and if so, decide now whether to descope, push part of it to Q4, or accept the $30,000 overage deliberately rather than discover it in October.
brief_head: **Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

system.text:
You are a rigorous, senior evaluator (a grader) for Lookout, a committed-spend finance agent. You judge whether ONE agent output — a "brief", which is either a FLAG or a deliberate SILENCE — is CORRECT for its scenario, strictly against a rubric. You are the skeptical reviewer; do not be generous.

You judge INTENT and reasoning, not surface wording. Consider: did the brief reach the correct call (flag / stay silent / flag-late / flag-uncertain)? If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

Return ONLY a JSON object, nothing else:
{"verdict": "PASS" | "PARTIAL" | "FAIL", "reasoning": "<2-4 sentences: what the brief did, measured against the rubric's pass/partial/fail lines, and why that verdict>"}

Never output a numeric score. PARTIAL is for the rubric's explicit partial case (right direction, a real flaw). Reserve PASS for meeting the pass bar; FAIL for the wrong call or a missing essential.

messages.content:

RUBRIC — what counts as correct for this scenario:
- PASS if: The agent FLAGS the budget (raises it as needing attention — states the true position is at/over plan, or clearly heading there — and does NOT stay silent). Reasoning is sound and grounded in the data.
- PARTIAL if: Flags, but with a real flaw: wrong framing/timing (calls a preventable overrun hopeless, or a too-late one preventable), or a shaky/ungrounded rationale.
- FAIL if: Stays SILENT (misses the overrun), OR asserts a position the data doesn't support.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — over plan now, ~1 month left to act**

Contract Development looks 45% spent. It's actually at 115% of plan.

The dashboard shows $180,000 paid out of a $400,000 quarter — looks comfortably under half. But there's $280,000 in signed, still-unbilled commitments sitting off to the side that the surface number doesn't reflect. Add it in and the true position is $460,000 against a $400,000 plan — $60,000 over, with one month left in the quarter.

The receipts:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pinegate Audit Associates | $180,000 | $90,000 | $90,000 | Contract on file |
| Cobalt Bridge Consulting | $150,000 | $60,000 | $90,000 | Contract on file |
| Harborview Staffing Solutions | $100,000 | $0 | $100,000 | **Email only — never logged as a contract** |

Two of these are partly-billed contracts, so finance has seen them before — but the Harborview piece is $100,000 fully unbilled and sourced only from an email, not the contract file. That's the one nobody would catch by looking at the system.

None of the $280,000 owed across these three has fully landed — Pinegate and Cobalt are only half-billed, Harborview hasn't been billed at all. That's still preventable money, but there's only one month left in the quarter for it to convert into actual charges, and once it does, this stops being a conversation about prevention and becomes one about explaining a $60,000 overrun after the fact.

Get Carlos Enriquez (Procurement Manager, budget owner) to confirm the Harborview engagement — get it logged properly and find out if the remaining $100,000 can be scoped down or deferred. Same question on the unbilled halves of Pinegate and Cobalt: are those invoices still coming this quarter as planned, or is there room to push them out? Decide now whether to cut scope on any of the three before the remaining $280,000 turns into paid invoices.

label: FLAG
brief:
Single reading only, so no trajectory call needed (has_trajectory would be false with one reading).

This is a clear flag — over plan, one month left, and one commitment never logged in the contract system.

---

**Flag — early, ~1 month to act**

Contract Development looks 45% spent. It's actually at 115% of plan.

The dashboard shows $180,000 paid out against a $400,000 quarterly plan — looks comfortably under half. But there's $280,000 in signed and promised work sitting off to the side that hasn't been billed yet. Add it in and the true position is $460,000 against a $400,000 plan — $60,000 over, with one month left in Q3.

Here's what's driving it:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still Owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pinegate Audit Associates | $180,000 | $90,000 | $90,000 | Contract on file |
| Cobalt Bridge Consulting | $150,000 | $60,000 | $90,000 | Contract on file |
| Harborview Staffing Solutions | $100,000 | $0 | $100,000 | **Email only — never logged in the contract system** |

The two logged contracts together account for $180,000 still owed. The Harborview piece — $100,000, fully unbilled — exists only as an email commitment. That's the one nobody would catch looking at contracts alone, and it's the single biggest unbilled slice.

None of that $280,000 has hit the account yet — it's all still preventable. But there's only one month left in the quarter, and once Pinegate and Cobalt bill out their remaining work and Harborview gets invoiced, this becomes actual cash out the door and the conversation shifts from "we caught this" to "we're explaining a $60,000 overrun."

Get Carlos Enriquez to confirm the Harborview commitment now — scope it down or push it to Q4 if it isn't essential this quarter — and get firm invoicing timelines from Pinegate and Cobalt before assuming any of that $180,000 in remaining contract work lands this quarter. One month is enough to renegotiate; it isn't enough to absorb a surprise.

label: SILENT
brief:
**Payroll & HR Outsourcing — watched, nothing to act on.**

True position is $160,000 against a $250,000 plan (64%), with one month left in Q3. The only open commitment is the Meridian Digital Agency contract — $100,000 signed, $80,000 already billed, just $20,000 left to bill — and it's properly on file (contract PDF), not a surprise. Surface dashboard shows 56%, true number 64%; the gap is small and fully explained by that one contract line. No email-only or unlogged commitments anywhere in this budget. Comfortably under plan — no action needed.

You judge INTENT and reasoning, not surface wording. Consider: did the brief reach the correct call (flag / stay silent / flag-late / flag-uncertain)? If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month of runway left**

Interim Executive Staffing looks 50% spent. It's actually at 117% of plan.

The dashboard shows $90,000 paid against a $180,000 budget — half gone, looks calm. What it doesn't show is a $120,000 commitment to Nexora Consulting Group that exists only as an email-sourced record, never logged as billed, sitting entirely unpaid. Add that in and the true position is $210,000 against a $180,000 plan — $30,000 over, with one month left in the quarter.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nexora Consulting Group | $120,000 | $0 | $120,000 | Email only — not in the contracts system (source: Nexora_Consulting_Group.pdf) |

That $120,000 is the whole story: it's a single, non-recurring, email-only recruiter commitment — not a logged contract — so nothing about it was going to surface on its own. Not one dollar of it has been billed yet, which is the good news: it's still unpaid, meaning it's still preventable if someone acts this month. Once invoices start landing against it, this stops being a conversation about prevention and becomes one about explaining a $30,000 overrun after the fact.

Go to Brian Kowalski (IT Director, budget owner) and get the Nexora engagement confirmed — scope, timing, and whether the full $120,000 is actually going to be billed this quarter or can be deferred/reduced. With one month left, the decision on whether to scale it back needs to happen now, before billing starts.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — late, already paid out**

DevOps Toolchain isn't hiding anything on a dashboard — the surface number and the true number are the same, and that's exactly the problem: it shows **111% of plan**, because it *is* 111% of plan. Plan was $180,000. Actual paid spend is **$200,000** — you're $20,000 over, and every dollar of it has already cleared the account. There's no unbilled pile lurking behind this one; there's just a hole that's already been dug, with one month left in the quarter.

What happened: two recurring annual renewals — Zendesk ($30,000) and HubSpot ($20,000) — both signed contracts, both fully billed and paid, landing right on schedule. Normally that kind of renewal is routine and doesn't warrant a flag. But paired with two other charges that ran through the same quarter — Zoom Basic and Adobe Creative Cloud, $75,000 each, neither tied to any logged commitment — the combination pushed total paid spend to $200,000 against a $180,000 plan. There's no committed-but-unbilled money left to worry about; committed remaining is $0. This is fully realized, fully paid overspend.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zendesk | $30,000 | $30,000 | $0 | Contract on file (Zendesk.pdf) |
| HubSpot | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Contract on file (HubSpot.pdf) |
| Zoom Basic | — | $75,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no commitment on file |
| Adobe Creative Cloud | — | $75,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no commitment on file |

Runway-wise, there's nothing left to prevent — the money moved in July and August, and one month remains in Q3 with committed remaining sitting at $0. This isn't a "catch it before it lands" situation; it's a "explain it and stop it recurring" one.

Two things to do now: go to Rachel Torres and get a straight answer on why Zoom Basic and Adobe Creative Cloud ran outside any logged commitment — those are the two charges that turned a routine renewal quarter into a breach, and they should be on contract or budgeted for next time. Separately, flag the $180,000 Q3 plan itself as too tight once these two annual renewals are baked in — either raise the plan or trim one of the untracked tools before Q4.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — late, already paid out**

DevOps Toolchain isn't hiding anything on a dashboard — the surface number and the true number are the same, and that's exactly the problem: it shows **111% of plan**, because it *is* 111% of plan. Plan was $180,000. Actual paid spend is **$200,000** — you're $20,000 over, and every dollar of it has already cleared the account. There's no unbilled pile lurking behind this one; there's just a hole that's already been dug, with one month left in the quarter.

What happened: two recurring annual renewals — Zendesk ($30,000) and HubSpot ($20,000) — both signed contracts, both fully billed and paid, landing right on schedule. Normally that kind of renewal is routine and doesn't warrant a flag. But paired with two other charges that ran through the same quarter — Zoom Basic and Adobe Creative Cloud, $75,000 each, neither tied to any logged commitment — the combination pushed total paid spend to $200,000 against a $180,000 plan. There's no committed-but-unbilled money left to worry about; committed remaining is $0. This is fully realized, fully paid overspend.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zendesk | $30,000 | $30,000 | $0 | Contract on file (Zendesk.pdf) |
| HubSpot | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Contract on file (HubSpot.pdf) |
| Zoom Basic | — | $75,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no commitment on file |
| Adobe Creative Cloud | — | $75,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no commitment on file |

Runway-wise, there's nothing left to prevent — the money moved in July and August, and one month remains in Q3 with committed remaining sitting at $0. This isn't a "catch it before it lands" situation; it's a "explain it and stop it recurring" one.

Two things to do now: go to Rachel Torres and get a straight answer on why Zoom Basic and Adobe Creative Cloud ran outside any logged commitment — those are the two charges that turned a routine renewal quarter into a breach, and they should be on contract or budgeted for next time. Separately, flag the $180,000 Q3 plan itself as too tight once these two annual renewals are baked in — either raise the plan or trim one of the untracked tools before Q4.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

brief:
**Flag — late; the overspend already happened**

Data Storage & Backup isn't heading toward a problem — it already has one. The dashboard shows spend at 107% of plan, and that's not a preview: the true position is the same number, $320,000 against a $300,000 plan, because every dollar of it has already been paid out. There's no hidden unbilled pile inflating this one; what you see is what actually moved.

Here's the shape of it: three annual tool renewals — GitHub Enterprise, Slack, and Zoom — landed and were paid the same week in August, totaling $60,000 signed and $60,000 billed, fully closed out, nothing owed. On top of that, two payments to DocuSign ($130,000) and Dropbox ($130,000) went out in July. Those two are pure paid transactions with no corresponding signed commitment on file anywhere in the contract records — they show up in the transaction ledger but were never logged as a commitment before the money moved.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GitHub Enterprise | $25,000 | $25,000 | $0 | Contract on file (GitHub_Enterprise.pdf) |
| Slack | $20,000 | $20,000 | $0 | Contract on file (Slack.pdf) |
| Zoom | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Contract on file (Zoom.pdf) |
| DocuSign | — | $130,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no logged commitment |
| Dropbox | — | $130,000 (paid) | — | Transaction only, no logged commitment |

Total paid to date: $320,000. Nothing left uncommitted or unbilled — the plan is over by $20,000, full stop. There's one month left in the quarter, but that runway doesn't help here: there's no unpaid commitment left to renegotiate, defer, or scope down. This isn't a "catch it before it lands" situation — it already landed.

Two things to do with this: first, go to Linda Nguyen (VP of IT Operations) and get confirmation on why DocuSign and Dropbox were paid without a signed commitment ever being logged — that's the real gap, not the amount. Second, flag the renewal timing for next year: three annual renewals clustering into the same week is what tipped this from comfortable to over, and that's schedulable — worth checking if GitHub, Slack, and Zoom can be staggered across the year so one week doesn't absorb three renewals at once. Nothing to claw back this quarter, but this is the one to walk in with before someone else notices the 107% first.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — late, breach already booked**

Collaboration Software shows 105.6% of plan on the dashboard. The true position is also 105.6%. There's no hidden unbilled pile here — the surprise isn't a gap between surface and true, it's that the money is *already spent* and the quarter isn't over.

Plan is $90,000. Actual cash paid out is $95,000. Nothing is sitting as committed-but-unbilled — committed remaining is $0 — so this isn't a "brace for what's coming" situation, it's "this already happened." The budget is over plan by $5,000, with 1 month left in Q3 2026.

Here's what makes up the $95,000:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Status | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Workday | $15,000 | $15,000 | $0 | Paid, annual renewal | Workday.pdf (contract) |
| SAP Concur | $10,000 | $10,000 | $0 | Paid, annual renewal | SAP_Concur.pdf (contract) |
| Dropbox | — | $35,000 paid | — | No commitment on file | transaction only |
| Grammarly Business | — | $35,000 paid | — | No commitment on file | transaction only |

Workday and SAP Concur are the routine annual renewals — signed contracts on file, fully billed, nothing outstanding. On their own they'd be unremarkable. But Dropbox and Grammarly Business — $35,000 each, fully paid — have no commitment record at all; they never went through the contract/commitment tracking, they just show up as paid transactions. Combined with the two renewals, that's what pushed a $90,000 budget to $95,000 with a month still to go.

There's nothing left to intercept this quarter — no unbilled commitment to renegotiate or defer, because it's all already cash-out-the-door. The runway question isn't "can we stop this" (we can't, it's paid), it's "will anything else land in the last month" — and two of the four charges here (Dropbox, Grammarly) came in with zero advance visibility in the tracked commitments, which is exactly the kind of spend that could repeat without warning.

For the controller: this isn't Michael Brooks needing to defer open contract work — there's no open contract work left to defer. The ask is different: get Dropbox and Grammarly Business onto the commitment/contract log going forward so they show up before they're paid, not after, and confirm with Michael Brooks whether either is itself a recurring charge that'll hit again before quarter close. Right now you're explaining a $5,000 overrun after the fact — the fix is making sure next quarter's version of Dropbox/Grammarly shows up as a commitment, not a surprise transaction.

Workday and SAP Concur are the routine annual renewals — signed contracts on file, fully billed, nothing outstanding. On their own they'd be unremarkable. But Dropbox and Grammarly Business — $35,000 each, fully paid — have no commitment record at all; they never went through the contract/commitment tracking, they just show up as paid transactions. Right now you're explaining a $5,000 overrun after the fact — the fix is making sure next quarter's version of Dropbox/Grammarly shows up as a commitment, not a surprise transaction.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Demand Gen (Q3 2026) — Flag, act now: ~1 month left before the rest of this bills automatically.**

The dashboard shows Demand Gen at 87% spent. It's actually at 107% of plan — already $10,000 over, with the quarter not even closed.

Here's the gap: $130,000 has been paid out so far. But there's a $150,000 contract with Catalyst Performance Media that's only been billed $120,000 of — leaving $30,000 still owed and coming. Add that to what's already paid and the true position is $160,000 against a $150,000 plan. The dashboard's 87% only reflects cash paid; it doesn't see the $30,000 still queued up on that contract.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalyst Performance Media | $150,000 | $120,000 | $30,000 | Signed contract on file (Catalyst_Performance_Media_agreement.pdf) |

This isn't a renewal and isn't winding down — it's an active, one-off commitment, fully logged on a signed contract, not something buried in email. But it is fully accounted for and confirmed, which is exactly why it's actionable: the $30,000 hasn't been charged yet, which means there's still a window to negotiate scope or timing before it lands.

You've got one month left in the quarter. Once that final $30,000 bills, this is locked in at $160,000 against a $150,000 plan — no longer preventable, just something to explain after the fact. Right now it still is preventable.

Get in front of it: go to Naomi Freid (VP Demand Generation) and confirm what that remaining $30,000 on the Catalyst contract actually covers — see if any of it can be scoped down, delayed into Q4, or held back before it's billed. Decide now whether to eat the $10,000 overage or push to trim it before month-end close.

**Marketing (Q3 2026) — Flag, early, ~1 month left to act**

Marketing looks 97.5% spent. It's actually at 104% of plan.

The dashboard shows $195,000 paid against a $200,000 plan — comfortably under, nothing to see. But there's $13,000 in signed, unbilled work sitting off that surface number: $10,000 still owed on the Highwire Brand Agency contract (an active campaign, not wrapping up) and $3,000 on a Nimbus Content Studio scope-of-work. Add that in and the true position is $208,000 — $8,000 over the $200,000 plan.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Highwire Brand Agency | $170,000 | $160,000 | $10,000 | Signed contract on file (Highwire_Brand_Agency_agreement.pdf) |
| Nimbus Content Studio | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Signed SOW on file (Nimbus_Content_Studio_SOW.pdf) |

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month of runway to act**

Data Warehouse & ETL looks 30% spent. It's actually at 105% of plan.

The dashboard shows $30,000 paid out of a $100,000 Q3 budget — comfortably under a third of the way through. What it doesn't show is a signed Zendesk contract for $75,000, still fully unbilled, sitting outside the paid total. Add that in and the true position is $105,000 — $5,000 over the full quarter's plan, with one month still left in Q3.

This is an annual renewal, and normally that's a shrug — routine, planned-for, nothing to chase. But this one pushes the budget over 100% of plan on its own, and the hard line here is simple: over plan is over plan, renewal or not.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zendesk | $75,000 | $0 | $75,000 | Signed contract on file (Zendesk.pdf) |

Nothing on the Zendesk piece has hit the account yet — that's the good news. It's fully preventable right now, but only for the one month left in the quarter; once it bills, this becomes a "why didn't we catch it" conversation instead of a "we caught it" one.

Get to Nicole Martinez now: confirm the Zendesk renewal terms, and check whether the $75,000 can be trimmed, delayed into Q4, or offset elsewhere before it bills. Decide this month — there's no runway left after that.
brief_head: **Flag — early, ~1 month of runway to act**

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Executive Offsite Budget looks 67% spent. It's actually at 108% of plan.

The dashboard shows $40,000 paid against a $60,000 plan — comfortably under two-thirds. What it doesn't show is $25,000 in signed travel and lodging commitments sitting unbilled, none of which have hit the account yet. Add that in and the true position is $65,000 — already $5,000 over the full quarter's plan, with one month still left in Q3.

Here's what's promised but not yet paid:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still Owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Southwest Airlines | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack thread |
| JetBlue Airways | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack thread |
| Marriott Hotels | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack thread |
| Hilton Hotels & Resorts | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack thread |
| Hyatt Regency | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack thread |
| Four Seasons Hotels | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | Email |

Five of the six live in Slack threads — all logged with confirmed vendor confirmations ($20,000 combined, per the tool's own subtotal). The Four Seasons piece — $5,000 — sits only in an email thread, never logged as a formal commitment; that's the one that would've been invisible without this check.

None of the $25,000 has been billed. That's the good news: every dollar of the overage is still preventable this month. Once these trips and hotel stays are invoiced, it's cash out the door and the conversation changes from "let's trim this" to "here's what happened."

Go to Michael Torres now, not after invoices land. Get confirmation on which of these six bookings are locked versus still adjustable — even trimming one hotel booking pulls this back under plan. Decide this week whether to cut, defer, or explicitly accept the $5,000 overage before the quarter closes.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

Grade it. Return only the JSON object.

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Engineering Team Retreat looks 70% spent. It's actually at 105% of plan.

The dashboard shows $70,000 paid out against a $100,000 budget — comfortable, two-thirds gone with a month left in Q3. What it doesn't show is $35,000 in signed hotel, car-rental, rideshare, and travel-booking commitments sitting in email, none of it billed yet. Add that in and the true position is $105,000 — $5,000 over plan — not the 70% the dashboard implies.

All ten of these commitments come from the same place: email, not the finance system. Nobody logged them. Each is small on its own ($3,500), which is exactly why they're easy to miss individually — but together they're the whole gap between what looks fine and what's actually over.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hilton Hotels & Resorts | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Hyatt Regency | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Four Seasons Hotels | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Westin Hotels | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Enterprise Rent-A-Car | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Hertz Car Rental | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Avis Budget Group | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Uber for Business | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Lyft Business | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |
| Expedia Corporate Travel | $3,500 | $0 | $3,500 | email only |

Not one dollar of that $35,000 has hit the account yet — that's the good news, and it's why this is still preventable. But there's only one month left in the quarter, and once these bills land, the $5,000 overage becomes real and locked in rather than something you caught in advance.

Get Jennifer Park (Chief of Staff, budget owner) to confirm which of these ten bookings are actually still happening — some retreat logistics may overlap or be cancellable — and get them logged into the finance system properly so they stop living only in inboxes. Decide now whether to trim one or two vendors to bring this back under $100,000, or accept the $5,000 overage and document why.

system.text:
You are a rigorous, senior evaluator (a grader) for Lookout, a committed-spend finance agent. You judge whether ONE agent output — a "brief", which is either a FLAG or a deliberate SILENCE — is CORRECT for its scenario, strictly against a rubric. You are the skeptical reviewer; do not be generous.

You judge INTENT and reasoning, not surface wording. Consider: did the brief reach the correct call (flag / stay silent / flag-late / flag-uncertain)? If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

Return ONLY a JSON object, nothing else:
{"verdict": "PASS" | "PARTIAL" | "FAIL", "reasoning": "<2-4 sentences: what the brief did, measured against the rubric's pass/partial/fail lines, and why that verdict>"}

Never output a numeric score. PARTIAL is for the rubric's explicit partial case (right direction, a real flaw). Reserve PASS for meeting the pass bar; FAIL for the wrong call or a missing essential.

=== AGENT OUTPUT TO GRADE (the brief) ===
**Board Meeting Travel — Flag, tight window, act now**

This budget looks 75% spent. It's actually at 108% of plan.

The dashboard shows $90,000 paid against a $120,000 plan — comfortably under. What it doesn't show is $40,000 in signed travel bookings that haven't been billed yet: nine vendor commitments, all still sitting as "promised," none of it charged. Add that to what's already paid and the true position is $130,000 against a $120,000 plan — $10,000 over, before a single one of those bookings has actually hit the account.

Eight of the nine live only in email confirmations, never logged as contracts in the finance system — this is the pile you'd never see just checking the dashboard:

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avis Budget Group | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |
| Uber for Business | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |
| Lyft Business | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |

| American Express GBT | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |
| BCD Travel | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |
| CWT (Carlson Wagonlit) | $4,500 | $0 | $4,500 | email |
| Airbnb for Work | $4,000 | $0 | $4,000 | Slack |

That's $36,000 sitting in email-only commitments and $4,000 in a Slack thread — $40,000 total, none of it billed yet.

Runway: this is Q3 2026, two of three months elapsed, one month left. Every one of these nine bookings is still unbilled — not one dollar of that $40,000 has moved yet — so it's technically still preventable. But with only one month left in the quarter, that window is closing fast, and once travel happens the invoices will land automatically. Wait, and you're the one explaining a 108%-of-plan overrun after the fact instead of the one who caught it with a month still on the clock.

Go to Amanda Rodriguez now with the list above — confirm which of these nine bookings are still needed for the board meeting, and get any that can be trimmed or pushed to Q4 canceled or rescoped before they bill. Even canceling one or two brings this back under plan.
=== END AGENT OUTPUT ===

Grade it. Return only the JSON object.
