brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 month left to act**

Customer Site Visits looks 75% spent. It's actually at 106% of plan.

The dashboard shows $60,000 paid against an $80,000 budget — comfortable, a quarter of runway left. But sitting behind that is $25,000 in signed travel-vendor commitments that haven't been billed yet, and none of it shows up in the 75% number. Add it in and the true position is $85,000 against an $80,000 plan — already $5,000 over, before a single one of those five invoices lands.

Every one of those five commitments came in through email, not the contracts system — nobody logged them anywhere else. That's exactly the kind of thing that doesn't show up until the invoice hits.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Expedia Corporate Travel | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | email only |
| Concur Travel | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | email only |
| American Express Global Business Travel | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | email only |
| BCD Travel | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | email only |
| CWT (Carlson Wagonlit Travel) | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | email only |
| Airbnb for Work | $5,000 | $5,000 | $0 | paid, closed out |

Only Airbnb has actually settled. The other $25,000 hasn't touched the account — which is the good news: it's still preventable. One month remains in the quarter, and once those five invoices come due, this stops being a conversation you can have and becomes one you're explaining after the fact.

Get Robert Nguyen to confirm which of these five are still needed this quarter versus deferrable — with the budget already $5,000 over plan before they're even billed, any one of them going through as scheduled locks in an overrun you'll have to account for. Better to trim or push now than explain it in October.

Customer Site Visits looks 75% spent. It's actually at 106% of plan.

If it stayed silent, was silence genuinely the right call? Is the reasoning sound and grounded? Did it overreach beyond the data it actually had (the ground truth is given)? Deterministic checks (no fabricated numbers, right budget) have already been run in code and passed — do not re-litigate those; focus on judgment.

Return ONLY a JSON object, nothing else:
{"verdict": "PASS" | "PARTIAL" | "FAIL", "reasoning": "<2-4 sentences: what the brief did, measured against the rubric's pass/partial/fail lines, and why that verdict>"}

Never output a numeric score. PARTIAL is for the rubric's explicit partial case (right direction, a real flaw). Reserve PASS for meeting the pass bar; FAIL for the wrong call or a missing essential.

brief:
**Recruiting Travel & Interviews — Flag, early but the window is one month wide**

This budget looks 60% spent. It's actually at 110% of plan.

David Kim's recruiting travel budget shows $30,000 paid against a $50,000 plan — a comfortable-looking dashboard. But there's $25,000 more in signed travel commitments sitting off that surface number, none of it billed yet. Add it in and the true position is $55,000 against a $50,000 plan — **$5,000 over**, with one month left in the quarter to do anything about it.

None of the $25,000 has hit the account. Every commitment below shows $0 billed. That's the good news — it's still preventable — and also the warning: it will stop being preventable the moment these vendors invoice.

| Vendor | Signed | Billed | Still owed | Where it's logged |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Westin Hotels | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Slack |
| Enterprise Rent-A-Car | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Slack |
| Hertz Car Rental | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Slack |
| Avis Budget Group | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Slack |
| Uber for Business | $3,000 | $0 | $3,000 | Slack |
| Lyft Business | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | Email only |
| Expedia Corporate Travel | $5,000 | $0 | $5,000 | Email only |

Five of those seven ($15,000 combined) were only ever captured in Slack threads. The other two — Lyft Business and Expedia Corporate Travel, $10,000 combined — exist only in an email thread, never logged as a formal commitment anywhere else. That $10,000 is the piece that could genuinely blindside you: nothing about it lives where anyone checks budget status.

None of this is a routine renewal — every one of these is a one-off, non-recurring travel commitment, and together they already put the quarter over plan with a month still to go.

Stakes: with one month left and zero of the $25,000 billed, you still have room to renegotiate or cut scope before it becomes cash out the door. Wait past this month and you're the one explaining a $5,000 overrun instead of the one who caught it.

What to do now: go to David Kim and get confirmation on which of these seven bookings are still changeable — especially the two email-only ones (Lyft, Expedia) that never made it into a tracked commitment. Either scale back what's not yet booked or flag to leadership now that this budget will close over plan.

The dashboard shows this budget 56% spent. It's actually at 111% of plan.

Two payments already cleared the account — $25,000 to Rapid Notary Solutions and $25,000 to GreenLeaf Cleaning Contractors, totaling the $50,000 shown as paid. That's the calm number Gregory's dashboard reflects. What it doesn't show is a signed $50,000 commitment to Ashworth Financial Advisory sitting in email — not logged anywhere in the finance system — with zero billed against it yet. Add that in and the true position is $100,000 against a $90,000 plan: $10,000 over, with one month left in the quarter.

|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rapid Notary Solutions | — | $25,000 (paid) | $0 | transaction record |
| GreenLeaf Cleaning Contractors | — | $25,000 (paid) | $0 | transaction record |

label: FLAG
brief:
**Flag — early, ~1 week to act**

Intent Data & Signals looks 78% spent through week four of a three-month quarter. That number alone isn't the problem — the climb underneath it is: this budget is accelerating toward a breach next week, not next quarter.

There are no signed commitments sitting off to the side here — every dollar so far is actual paid spend (a single Canva Pro line, $78,000 cumulative through July 27). So the surface and the true position are the same number today: $78,000, 78% of the $100,000 plan. The catch isn't a hidden pile — it's the rate of climb.

Week by week: $15,000 → $32,000 → $52,000 → $78,000. That's deltas of $17,000, then $20,000, then $26,000 — each week bigger than the last, officially "accelerating." At the recent pace ($26,000/week), the next reading is projected at $104,000 — over the $100,000 full-quarter plan, with two months still left to run in the quarter. That's one week away.

| Week ending | True position | % of plan |
|---|---|---|
| Jul 6 | $15,000 | 15% |
| Jul 13 | $32,000 | 32% |
| Jul 20 | $52,000 | 52% |
| Jul 27 | $78,000 | 78% |

Nothing here is a signed contract or a commitment sitting unbilled — it's straight spend, so there's no invoice sitting in a drawer to intercept. That actually makes this more urgent, not less: there's no vendor to renegotiate a payment schedule with, only a spend rate to slow down before it clears $100K with two-thirds of the quarter still to go.

Get to Priya Nair on what's driving the weekly jump in this line — whether it's a one-time ramp (event push, data buy) that will taper, or a new steady-state cost that's simply outgrowing the quarterly plan. Decide now whether to cap spend for the next few weeks or request a plan increase before the number itself forces the conversation.
